text: Hello, This is a case of laparoscopic repair of incisional hernia. This patient was having the previous paraumbilical hernia and for that he was repaired by the open surgery without mesh and now he has a re-occurrence.
The laparoscope is inserted through the Palmer's point, providing a view inside the abdomen where the omentum is minimally adhered to the anterior abdominal wall.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using a harmonic scalpel to carefully separate the omentum from the anterior abdominal wall. The previous proline suture causing recurrent stitch abscess is being removed.

Laparoscopic ventral hernia repair is the gold standard and in our practice we have used the different type of mesh like omega-three fatty acid treated mesh, proceed mesh, Dacron mesh, polyurethane mesh, Wipro mesh and even the proline mesh. Proline mesh also has a equally good effect. and we have used more than two hundred cases of the simple polypropylene mesh in a patient who is socio-economically poor class and we have the good result. In our opinion, using a very expensive mesh is not going to help and there is no guarantee that adhesion will not develop. So, even in the IPOM technique, use of polypropylene mesh should be considered.

If you will mobilize the patient early and if you will not do a lot of dissection, if you will not have a hematoma, then the adhesion chances are negligible. Although if adhesion happens also, some omental adhesion can be expected. So in the patient who cannot afford the expensive mesh, we have used the simple polypropylene mesh and we have a good result.

The surgeon performs additional adhesiolysis while an assistant reduces the hernia bulge from above, allowing access to the hernia sac contents. The team checks for any omental bubble trapped between the loops of the omentum.

The surgeon carefully separates the periphery of the adhesion, allowing the omentum within the hernia sac to be released. The assistant aids from above, utilizing gravity to assist in the maneuver. This laparoscopic procedure is performed using only two ports, with the harmonic device as the sole instrument inside the abdomen.

The surgeon separates the remaining omentum, pulling out some fat content that belongs to the peritoneum rather than the omentum. A visible knot of proline, which has caused a recurrent stitch abscess, is identified and cut for removal.

The surgeon identifies and pulls out a knot associated with a stitch abscess. A 15x15 cm mesh is prepared for placement, and its corners are fixed using trans-facial fixation, with prior mapping of the mesh on the skin.

The surgeon marks a point approximately two centimeters lateral to the corners of the mesh and creates a small two-millimeter prick. A suture passer or varus needle is used to pass the pre-tied vicryl sutures through the mesh corners. The vicryl number one is chosen for its absorbable properties, reducing nerve irritation and pain compared to proline.

The surgeon fixes the lateral two corners of the mesh with trans-facial sutures, followed by securing the other two lateral sutures towards the surgeon. Careful mapping is performed to avoid pricking the inferior epigastric vessels and to identify dangerous areas such as the triangle of doom, triangle of pain, and trapezoid of disaster.

The surgeon ensures the iliac and inferior epigastric vessels are not compromised during the ventral hernia repair. The third corner of the mesh is visible, stretched nicely to prevent corrugation or loose hanging. The mesh is positioned two centimeters lateral to maintain tension even after the removal of pneumoperitoneum gas, preventing shrinkage.

The surgeon uses a simple polypropylene mesh without coating, similar to that used in open surgery. The fourth corner of the mesh is being taken out, leaving some rectus muscle between the pricks. The knot is tied subcutaneously, ensuring it holds the rectus and muscle securely.

The surgeon tightens all corners of the polypropylene mesh, ensuring it is completely stretched and symmetrically placed over the hernia site. The ProTac auto suture tacker is used to fix the mesh at the corners, maintaining a two-centimeter distance between tacks for both outer and inner crowning.

And very same day we will discharge the patient. From next day patient can have the normal activity in the home and after one week in the office. Generally what we recommend the patient that patient should not have the same posture for long time and he should try to encourage walking. Of course abdominal belt is good practice for at least two weeks postoperatively that will support the abdominal wall and excessive pressure can be avoided.
The surgeon identifies the falciform ligament, which is left intact during the procedure. In cases of epigastric hernia, the falciform ligament may be dissected before placing the mesh.

The surgeon ensures that the mesh projects at least six centimeters beyond the healthy margin of the defect to prevent recurrence. The inner crowning of the mesh is complete, and the surgery concludes with the gradual removal of gas. A ten-millimeter port is placed at the palmar point, and a five-millimeter port is positioned in the lumbar area on the left side.
So thank you very much for watching this simple surgery of the laparoscopic repair of incisional hernia.
